speaker: interviewer
text: This is the beginning of the tape 3, interview of Eva Safferman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one day, the German man asked my mother, is it true that you were all prostitute? And my mother says, OK, I grant you, I might have been. But how about my daughter? She is just a child. How could she be anything? So he says, yes, I understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: And every day when we were walking from work to the-- to the trains, I would look and see buildings with lights in 'em and people living like-- like human beings. And I would think to myself, will I ever live like this? Will I-- will I always be walking around in five people in a row? Would it ever happen, you know? It was just-- it was just like you weren't a human being. You were just part of a five. And there were all women--

speaker: interviewee
text: All women.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in this camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just women, just women. We were just women.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were an adolescent, but there were no boys around for you to talk to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in any event.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no boys, no men, nobody, no. It was just women. It was a lot of women. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what happened when the-- there was one day we were on a working-- working, and there was a air raid. So they put us into a bunker, and we were all hiding in that bunker, and there was this-- this German with us. And you heard bombs falling all around us, and of course, we were praying, you know, to God. And all through the war, I remembered my uncle's address, you know, from America. I don't know. I just remembered it. And when we walked out from the bunker after there was the all clear after the air raid, when we walked out there was a bomb in front of our bunk, a-- a-- a-- a big hole in the-- in-- in the back all around. And the German says, you know, must have been because of you people that we were spared. And we went back, and then we heard that they said that it-- they're going to close the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the-- not the name of the commander. You didn't know that. But what did he look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [SIGHS] German, I don't know, with a little mustache with glasses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tall, short?

speaker: interviewee
text: Medium, medium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Medium--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --build as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Because every day, we used to stay every morning and every night. They used to count us. They used to count and count, and we were standing and standing. Some people-- oh, yeah, I was talking about that slaughterhouse. So there was a lot of-- you know, like cows sometimes, they would lose their placenta. And this was meat, and people-- women were eating it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one woman once grabbed this piece of meat, and she hid it on her. And of course, when we were coming back, they-- we used to be searched. And when we used to be searched, they found-- this woman dropped this piece of meat. So they saw the meat on the floor, so they started yelling, who dropped it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, nobody would say who dropped it. And so we were standing a half a night. Some people already fainted, some women. So they were hit, and they had to get up again. And you know, because they wanted to know who dropped this. So this was one of the worst nights that we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ever find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Nobody ever told us. That's why we had stay almost all night long at that appell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever hit?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was never hit. My mother was, though. They found a little piece of-- of a leaf of something that she had in her bag, and this Jewish kapo hit her. Oh, I wanted to kill her, but I just couldn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Same Czech woman that you spoke of before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was a-- this was a Polish woman. Her name was [? Mirka. ?] But she didn't really hit her this hard. She just was making believe, because she was one of the good ones. And we-- they decided that they going to ship us to-- take us out from Poppenbuttel Sasel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put us on train, and sure enough, this woman, there was a friend of this German tried to escape. But she had no place where to go, and after two days she submitted back to the camp. Yeah. And they allowed her to come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, what else would they do with her? So we went on trains again, and we were going towards Bergen-Belsen. We were going on the train, and then they had to descend the train, and we went on a march. They used to call it death march. We used to walk and walk. And on the way we saw some French soldiers and Italian soldiers they were. Some of them, if they had something, they would give people to eat. But we were walking and walking, and walk-- some people couldn't walk, so they hit them over the head. And I don't know. I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans hit them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I don't know with what strength me and my mom walked. I really don't know. It was just--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was hours that we walked. And coming-- you know, we were still a while away from the camp, and my mom said to us, with the five-- well, we always walked five-- and she says, you know what? I smell dead bodies. And everybody said to my mom, you're crazy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we came into Bergen-Belsen, and the first thing we saw was a mountain full of skeletons. It was a-- a sight that you could never forget. And from far, you saw people dragging the-- the skeletons. Because everybody looked like death, you know? Nobody-- all you could see was bones. I looked at my mother. She was just all bones. Everybody, you know. And we went and they put us in one big room. And it was so-- the whole camp in one room. Can you imagine this? I mean, we couldn't even move. How many people? Any idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it must be 500 or more.

speaker: interviewer
text: You talked about the five of you-- you, your mother, and three other women.

speaker: interviewee
text: Three other women. There was every time somebody else, but you have to be five. You couldn't go anyplace unless you were five in a row. You couldn't. This was it. The only thing, in the morning you could go to take a shower, that had showers in the camp. But so we were there overnight.

speaker: interviewer
text: At Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to get there? How long were you marching?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- as I told you, I-- I don't remember. I kind of blocked stuff out from my memory, because it was easier this way. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we were in Bergen-Belsen. And at night, I wanted to lay down, was dark, but nobody would let me. And my mother was-- I couldn't even get to my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I laid down on a woman and she says, [? Bascia? ?] And I said, yeah, because I-- I-- if she knew it was somebody else she would have pushed me away. This way she thought it was her sister. She let me sleep almost on top of her, you know? So, and then in the morning, towards the morning, we heard somebody knocking at the door, and a woman yells-- you want to hear about that? Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The woman knocks on the door and she yells-- this-- this Jewish kapo, her name was [? Mirka-- ?] and she says, [? Mirka, ?] open the door. I'm having a baby. So everybody said, oh, she probably flipped out. She's crazy. I mean, how could she have a baby? And she insisted-- please open it up. I'm-- I have-- I'm having a baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they finally opened the door, and they let her in. Sure enough, the woman was having a baby. Because from the time we came from the ghetto until we got to Bergen-Belsen, it was nine month. And this woman was walking around. She's got a swollen stomach, and she's going to die because the stomach is grouching. And she hears noise in her stomach, and she's going to die.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she's-- so this woman really had a baby. So she delivered the baby, and somebody had a rusted scissors, and they cut the umbilical cord. And they called the German, and they took her out. And I never forget this woman was laying there, and they put her in some kind of bed. I don't know. It was in a different room. I don't know how I remember that. But they gave her a whole pail of soup, and she ate the whole thing up. I don't understand how-- I don't know what happened to the baby, whether they lived, because we were so-- we weren't concerned with anybody else. Everybody was just-- to survive, and you know, everybody was really self-centered. Everybody wanted to survive. And many a times, my mom would give me a piece of her bread, and I really shouldn't have taken it, but I did. [SOBBING] Because I was so hungry. But she was-- she would-- she would share anything with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were lucky to be together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I feel very lucky. And all the time, I only-- I only pray to God I should be-- I should be freed on my birthday, and my wish was granted. We were freed on April 15, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who freed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The English, the British. They came, and they started saying, hello, hello, with those loudspeakers. We are here. The war is over. But you have to stay here another three days, then we'll be able to get you out. And there was a lot of people that, in those three days, got killed because they run to the gates, and the gates were still electrocuted. So they would just-- you could see them stuck to the gates. And a lot of people were shot, too. The Hungarians-- Hungarian military were still around, and if somebody wanted to escape they would-- shot them. There was just-- a lot of people died yet. And I remember that the kapo, the Czech kapo, she liked me, and she knew it was my birthday. And she gave me an extra-- there was a soup that we used to get still there, one soup. And she gave it to me and she says she wishes all my life I should have such lucky days as today. She was-- she was nice. She liked me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did the soldiers tell you that you had been liberated in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was Polish. It must have been Polish and English. And-- and every language, they repeated it, because there was a lot of inmates. We were not only Poles there. There were all kinds of-- there were not only all Jews there. There was also Gentile people in this camp, too. And we found out that the bread that they had in Bergen-Belsen was-- was-- had inside had ground glass. That's what they were supposed to feed us with. And also that the camp was on a time bomb, which they didn't have time to execute because they ran away. As soon as they saw that the British arrived, all those Germans ran away, and they just left us there. See, they wanted to bomb us, not to-- not to leave any-- any-- any sign of-- of their atrocities. You know, they wanted to hide it. But they didn't have the time, because it was too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had happened to the mountain of bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What had happened to the mountain of bodies that you first saw when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How high was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was still there. Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How high was it? You talked about a mountain.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was-- oh, very high, very high. I would say, like, four stories high. My husband was one of the ones that was dragging those-- those bodies into the-- onto the mountains, oh, into the-- the hole. It was-- it was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know your husband at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I didn't. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after you were liberated? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the liberate-- we were liberated, we-- we stayed the two days, and then the British came. And people were running because there was places where there was watches and stuff that they robbed from the Jews. I only went to look a pair of shoes, because I had no shoes. Because I was a little girl, I didn't know to go for-- for gold or for-- for money or for jewelry. I went to look for a pair of shoes. But they put us into-- in apartments houses nearby. People that were sick went to hospitals, and we went to the apartments there. And it's kind of unclear. I remember there was one-- one British soldier gave me chocolate, and he liked me. And I told him that I live someplace else, because I didn't want him to come. Because I didn't-- I was-- I was a young kid. I didn't want to have nothing to do with anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we lived in this-- in this place in Germany, not far from Bergen-Belsen. And then we found out that people, they had diphtheria. My mother was sick. She-- she got diphtheria. And she-- we were also covered with lice, which was from hunger and from dirt because we couldn't bathe or-- or clean ourselves anymore. So we heard that they take people that were sick into Sweden. So my mom decided that we should go to Sweden. So we applied, and we got our papers, whatever we had, and we were going on the way to Sweden. So whatever we had, they took everything away. They washed us. They quarantined us, and they gave us fresh clothes. And we went on a boat from Lubeck to-- to-- from Lubeck to Sweden. And we came to Malmo, and they put us into a museum, because that was a big place and we stayed there. Still unclear, I don't remember that much.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I remember from Sweden, I wrote a letter to my aunt and uncle in America. And they-- they moved in the interim. And fortunately, a neighbor got the letter and met my uncle, and he gave him the letter. And immediately, he sent a telegram to Sweden that he's happy that we are alive, [SOBBING] and he's going to be looking for anybody else that's alive. And he's going to try to send us papers. We should come to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel then after the war had ended and you were in Sweden with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I felt great. I-- I was happy. I knew that we're going to survive. I just knew it. I mean, I had such strange-- strong feeling that we're going to survive, and I was with my mom. And after we were there about two weeks in Malmo, this-- they send us to Boras. This was a little, like a-- a little seaside village in Sweden. And some people were put up in hotels. Some people were put up in barracks. We were lucky. Me and my mom stayed at a house of a director of the post office. And she was very good to us. She used to bring us breakfast in bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. She was not Jewish. But he liked us, and she really--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think she-- her name was Svensen. I have someplace a picture of her. I don't know where it is now. But she used to, you know, bring us treats. And-- and after being there a short while, my mom went to a, like, convalescent home, and I went to school. We went to school. And we were like-- sleep-- we slept there, and we lived there. We were in school, and we learned languages-- English and Swedish and arithmetic and Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got to eat some food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We got to eat some food. Yes, we did. We did. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Sweden?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Sweden three and a half years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you meet someone there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my husband in Sweden, yes. Yeah, we met in--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry. He was in a place that was-- in Sweden, they got like a [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is a place that they work together, and they pool-- like in Israel. They pool their earnings, and they live together. Some people cook, and some people go to work. And he stayed with them because he-- he wanted to go to Israel with the rest of the-- the people. But then he met me, and he change his mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: And decided to come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you arrange that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we send him-- after we were in America?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. How did you arrange to get him to America? Did you get married in Sweden or did you get married in--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We got married in the United States. My uncle send him papers. First he send us the papers. But in Sweden, we had one of the happiest times of our life. We were really, really free of any worries and-- and was-- the people were great, were very nice. Swedish people were wonderful. And I was in school, and on vacation they used to send us to visit our parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And once I got a-- a-- a letter from my mother, and she-- it was addressed [? Congo. ?] So I said, oh, my-- my-- my god, they send it to Africa? But this was [? Congo ?] in Sweden. And I went to visit her, and she was doing quite well. And after being there in-- in school, I was about a year and a half, we went to work. So we went to-- oh, no. The place that we stayed after Malmo was [? Mela. ?] This was a resort. And from [? Mela ?] we went to Boras-- from-- we went to school. To school, I was in [NON-ENGLISH]. I think so. I'm not sure now. Yeah, I think so. And then we stayed in school. And we went skiing, and we had fun. And we were singing, and we were-- it was great. It was really nice. We had a great time. And you had physically recovered?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Everybody wasn't skinny like anything because we had food. We could eat as much as we could. Though I think Sweden still was-- didn't have everything as abundant that they-- later on. In the beginning was a little bit, but we still had food.

speaker: interviewer
text: You learned that Henry had been at Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Henry has been in Bergen-Belsen, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the same time that you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was freed also on the same-- on my birthday he was freed. But he was sick. He went to a hospital, I understand, was very lucky to be alive, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to America physically? By boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went-- yeah, on the Yeah, we went on the Me and my mom and my girlfriend that's here in this, we went to-- on the And they were great. They went out, and they ate, and they took all the movies. And I was sick like a dog after, you know, that I really could eat everything I could. I couldn't because I was seasick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as a matter of fact, that steward that came in says to me, oh, you're just making it up, he says. So I said, if you only knew how I feel, you wouldn't say it. And the next day, he comes with a long nose and he tells me, oh, you must have cursed me. Because the first time-- he's a seaman for 15 years, and he was seasick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- were the seas rough?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came in January. We came January 16, 1949, and we came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you land?

speaker: interviewee
text: We land in New York, and my cousin-- my cousin Rene's husband, Dave, picked us up. And we-- we went to the Bronx where my uncle lived, and we stayed with them for about six month.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the uncle who's 102 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who is 102 years now, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Isaac, Isaac, Isaac Berman, yes. They were very nice to us. We started working. And they had a factory, and we worked for my aunt, me and my mom. And I went to school at night, because my mom said, you got to go to school because you got to learn. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then after '49, Henry came. We used to correspond. And he came in 1951, and that's when we got married. On June 23, 1951 we got married. And of course, there was a little struggle with me and my mom, because, you know, it was hard to make ends meet, because we started, like, from the scratch. But [BACKROUND NOISE] eventually, you know, we-- my mom always lived with us, even after we got married, because I wouldn't let my mom go anyplace. And we lived in the Bronx in a very nice building.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And after I went to school, I went to look for a job. And I got a job in an office, after I could speak English. Because my girlfriend said to me, why do you have to work in a factory? And I saw she was right, and I went and worked in an office. And [? gradual ?] worked myself up to be a full-charge bookkeeper. But of course, in the meanwhile, I had my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Lori.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lori was born on April 17, 1956.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you had another--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I--

speaker: interviewer
text: --child?

speaker: interviewee
text: --had my son, who was born on April 15, on my birthday, in 1959. And I was home for 11 years. I didn't work till they went to school full time. And they always had my mother to come home, so they didn't come to an empty apartment. It was-- it was tough. Henry had to work three jobs. But eventually, you know, we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have a grandchild or so.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- yeah, I have two grandchildren. I have Rachel and I have Brooke, which is two and a half, and Rachel is 11. And my-- my son-- my daughter is a-- you want to know what they do or you don't?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure I do.

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter is a psychiatric social worker, but now she's with the school-- board of education, and my son is a doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a psychiatrist, but right now he's working for a pharmaceutical company. And thank god we survived, and we're here.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you had to guess or have an opinion, which I suppose you do, as to why you survived, what would that opinion be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just destiny. It was just-- I had-- we had years to live, and we lived. I strongly believe in whatever is destined for you, that's what's going to happen. You had to be in the right place at the right time, and you have to be lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think can be done to prevent [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a horror like the Holocaust from occurring again?

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to educate people, to tell them what happened. It should never happen again. Because it can happen to anybody. It's not only the Jews. Another time, they might pick another, you know, another race, another religion and-- and say you decided this is not good and this is not right. That's why people have to be educated to know that everybody is-- is equal. Everybody is a person. Everybody has a right to live and to exist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you ever afraid now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm afraid now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes when I read the paper and I see what's happening, I-- I-- I fear that, God forbid, could-- could happen again. But I hope to God it won't, that people should hear and-- and-- and learn of whatever happened in the past and should learn from it and be more tolerant of other people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because, you know, you never know. You never know what-- what's coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never do.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
